name: Stripe
interestingFact: Stripe processes hundreds of billions of dollars per year and their API docs are legendary — they spent months on documentation before writing a single line of product code.
businessModel: Takes a percentage of each payment processed through its APIs

name: Databricks
interestingFact: Databricks was founded by the creators of Apache Spark, Delta Lake, and MLflow — three of the most important open-source data tools of the last decade.
businessModel: Cloud platform subscription — unified analytics on top of cloud data lakes

name: Cloudflare
interestingFact: Cloudflare's network sits in front of ~20% of all internet traffic and can absorb DDoS attacks over 2 Tbps — larger than most countries' total internet bandwidth.
businessModel: Freemium network services — CDN, DDoS protection, Zero Trust, Workers platform

name: Netflix
interestingFact: Netflix pioneered the chaos engineering discipline — they intentionally killed random production services to build resilience. Their tool Chaos Monkey became the gold standard for reliability testing.
businessModel: Subscription streaming — content creation and licensing

name: Confluent
interestingFact: Confluent was founded by the creators of Apache Kafka while at LinkedIn. Kafka was originally built to handle LinkedIn's 1 trillion events per day — more than the NYSE's trading volume.
businessModel: Managed Kafka-as-a-service plus enterprise Kafka licensing

name: Discord
interestingFact: Discord migrated from Go to Rust for their Read States service and saw CPU usage drop by 5x and latency become dramatically more consistent — one of the most cited real-world Rust success stories.
businessModel: Freemium — Nitro subscriptions, server boosts

name: Uber
tagline: Moving the world, for the better
interestingFact: Uber's Marketplace team runs a dynamic pricing algorithm (surge pricing) that needs to solve a global supply-demand balancing problem in under 200ms, across millions of simultaneous events.
businessModel: Marketplace commission on rides and deliveries

name: CockroachDB
interestingFact: CockroachDB is named after cockroaches because it's designed to survive anything. It uses the Raft consensus protocol across geographic regions to provide strong consistency with global distribution.
businessModel: Cloud-hosted distributed SQL-as-a-service plus enterprise licenses

name: HashiCorp
interestingFact: HashiCorp's suite of tools (Terraform, Vault, Consul, Nomad) became the de facto standard for infrastructure-as-code. Terraform alone has over 3,000 providers covering almost every cloud service.
businessModel: Open-core — enterprise features and managed cloud offerings on top of OSS tools

name: Vercel
interestingFact: Vercel created Next.js and pioneered the Jamstack deployment model. Their edge network runs React Server Components and streaming SSR closer to users than any traditional CDN.
businessModel: Freemium frontend deployment platform — pay for team features and usage

name: Linear
tagline: The issue tracker built for modern software teams
interestingFact: Linear is obsessed with performance — their app opens in under 100ms because they sync the entire issue database locally and run all queries client-side using SQLite in the browser via OPFS.
businessModel: SaaS subscription for engineering teams

name: Figma
interestingFact: Figma's real-time collaboration engine is built on CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) compiled to WebAssembly — they moved the rendering and sync logic from JavaScript to WASM for a 3x performance improvement.
businessModel: SaaS — free tier, pro per-seat subscription

name: Temporal
interestingFact: Temporal's workflow engine makes it possible to write long-running, fault-tolerant business logic as regular code. A workflow can sleep for 30 days, survive server crashes, and resume exactly where it left off.
businessModel: Open-core — managed Temporal Cloud plus enterprise on-prem

name: PlanetScale
interestingFact: PlanetScale's non-blocking schema changes are built on Vitess — the same MySQL sharding system that powers YouTube. Schema migrations at PlanetScale never lock tables, even on billion-row databases.
businessModel: Serverless MySQL-as-a-service with branching workflows

name: Fly.io
tagline: Run your apps close to your users
interestingFact: Fly.io runs applications inside Firecracker microVMs (the same technology AWS Lambda uses) on bare metal servers worldwide. Apps can boot in ~150ms and live-migrate between regions with zero downtime.
businessModel: Pay-per-use compute + bandwidth for containerized apps at the edge

name: Anthropic
interestingFact: Anthropic pioneered Constitutional AI — a technique where an AI model critiques and revises its own outputs based on a set of principles, rather than relying entirely on human feedback. This dramatically reduced the amount of human labeling needed.
businessModel: API access to Claude models plus enterprise contracts

name: GitHub
interestingFact: GitHub Actions runs over 2 million CI/CD workflows per day. Their merge queue feature processes commits using a technique similar to optimistic locking — it speculatively merges PRs and rolls back on failure.
businessModel: Freemium SaaS — Teams and Enterprise plans, Actions minutes

name: Hugging Face
tagline: The AI community building the future
interestingFact: Hugging Face's `transformers` library has been downloaded over 100 million times. The Hub hosts over 1 million public models — it's effectively the NPM registry of the AI world.
businessModel: Open-core — Hub hosting plus Inference Endpoints and Enterprise Hub

name: Shopify
interestingFact: Shopify handles the entire Black Friday / Cyber Monday surge — often 100x normal traffic — without pre-provisioning. They built a flash sale system using leaky bucket rate limiting that throttles buyers instead of crashing.
businessModel: SaaS subscription plus payment processing and merchant services

name: Tailscale
tagline: A mesh VPN that just works
interestingFact: Tailscale built their entire product on top of the open-source WireGuard protocol but added a control plane using the Noise protocol. Every device gets a unique IP in a 100.x.x.x range regardless of where it is in the world.
businessModel: Freemium — personal use free, team and enterprise plans
